mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Georgia on?
mapQuestions.answer: Asia, in the South Caucasus, although Georgia feels culturally somewhere between Europe and Asia. It borders Russia to the north and Turkey, Armenia and Azerbaijan to the south 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Georgia?
mapQuestions.answer: Tbilisi, on the Mtkvari river. About 1.1 million people. Famous for its old town of carved-wooden balconies, sulphur baths, and a recent boom of contemporary architecture.

mapQuestions.question: What language does Georgia speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Georgian, which has its OWN unique alphabet (one of just 14 alphabets in active use anywhere on Earth). The script looks like nothing else and is recognised by UNESCO as cultural heritage.

mapQuestions.question: Where does wine come from?
mapQuestions.answer: Georgia! Archaeologists have found 8,000-year-old wine residue in Georgian clay vessels, the oldest evidence of wine-making anywhere on Earth. Georgians ferment wine in big earthenware jars called "qvevri" buried in the ground.

mapQuestions.question: How mountainous is Georgia?
mapQuestions.answer: Two-thirds of the country is mountain. The Greater Caucasus range (along the Russian border) has peaks over 5,000 m, including Shkhara (5,193 m), Georgia's highest.

foods.name: Khinkali
foods.description: Georgian soup dumplings: thick twisted pleats of dough wrapped around a meat-and-broth filling. You hold the top knot, bite a small hole, slurp the broth, then eat the dumpling. The knot is NOT eaten.

foods.name: Khachapuri
foods.description: Boat-shaped Georgian cheese bread, with melted cheese inside, sometimes topped with a runny egg and a slab of butter in the centre (the "Adjarian" style). One of the most loved dishes anywhere.

foods.name: Mtsvadi
foods.description: Smoky pork or lamb skewers grilled over wood embers, served with onions, pomegranate seeds, and tkemali (a sour-plum sauce). The Georgian BBQ.

foods.name: Badrijani nigvzit
foods.description: Slices of fried aubergine rolled around a walnut-garlic-coriander paste, topped with pomegranate seeds. The Georgian starter you will dream about later.

foods.name: Churchkhela
foods.description: "Georgian Snickers": walnuts threaded onto a string and dipped repeatedly into thickened grape juice. Hangs in shop windows all across the country, looks like long candy sausages.

foods.name: Pkhali
foods.description: Cold vegetable patties of spinach, beetroot, leek or aubergine mashed with walnut-garlic-coriander paste, topped with pomegranate. A Georgian-table essential.

scavengerItems.label: The Narikala Fortress watching over Tbilisi from above the river
A carved-wooden balcony on a Tbilisi old-town house
The sulphur baths of Abanotubani (Tbilisi means "warm place" because of these natural hot springs)
A small hilltop Orthodox monastery with a conical stone roof
A "qvevri" (a giant clay wine jar) buried in the ground at a Georgian winery
Snow-capped Greater Caucasus mountains from Kazbegi or Mestia
Gergeti Trinity Church on a hillside with Mount Kazbek (5,047 m) behind it
Boat-shaped khachapuri being baked in a "tone" wood-fired oven
Bunches of churchkhela candy sausages hanging in a shop window
A medieval Svan defensive tower (Mestia village has 200+ of these stone towers)
A Tushetian shepherd dog on a high mountain pass
Three Georgian men singing traditional polyphonic harmony together

geoMatches.description: The capital. Carved-wooden balconies of the old town, hilltop fortress, sulphur baths, and a recent wave of bold modern buildings (Bridge of Peace, Public Service Hall). Cheap and surprisingly cosmopolitan.
A village in the Greater Caucasus mountains, 1,700 m up. Famous for Gergeti Trinity Church (14th century) sitting alone on a hillside with the 5,047 m Mount Kazbek behind it.
A high mountain town in Svaneti, with 200+ medieval stone defensive towers built by Svan families to hide in during raids. UNESCO World Heritage.

Georgia's ancient former capital, just outside Tbilisi. The Svetitskhoveli Cathedral has been a sacred site since the 4th century, when Georgia became one of the first countries to adopt Christianity (337 AD).
Georgia's main wine region, in the east. Rolling vineyards, hilltop monasteries, family wineries pressing grapes in qvevri jars buried in the ground.
A 12th-century cave city carved into a cliff face in the south. 13 levels of caves, churches and chambers, originally home to 50,000 people.

senses.smell: Hot khachapuri cheese bread fresh from a tone oven, smoky mtsvadi skewers grilling over wood, walnut-garlic paste, sulphur drifting from Tbilisi baths, ripe pomegranates in autumn markets…
senses.hear: "Gamarjoba!", three-part Georgian polyphonic singing in a basement restaurant, the call to prayer in the Tbilisi old town's small mosque, river water under the Bridge of Peace, mountain wind in Kazbegi…
senses.taste: Salty stretchy melted cheese with a soft egg yolk on top, smoky lamb skewers, sweet-sour tkemali plum sauce, the ancient deep flavour of qvevri wine, walnut-coated churchkhela…
senses.feel: Hot sulphur water in a Tbilisi bath, soft wool carpets in a mountain guesthouse, the cool of a 12th-century cave monastery, the bumpy ride up the military road to Kazbegi, deep stone of Mtskheta cathedral…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How old is Georgian wine-making?
stories.body: In 2017, archaeologists confirmed 8,000-year-old wine residue inside clay vessels found near Tbilisi. That makes Georgia the OLDEST place on Earth where wine was made. Georgians ferment their wine the original way: not in barrels or steel tanks, but in giant clay pots called "qvevri", buried up to the neck in the earth. The grapes (skin, seeds, stems and all) go in. The qvevri is sealed. The mixture ferments naturally for 6 months. The result is a deeply complex orange-brown wine completely unlike most European wines. UNESCO put traditional qvevri winemaking on its "intangible cultural heritage" list in 2013. Around 500 indigenous grape varieties grow only in Georgia, more than almost any country on Earth.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A way of making something has stayed almost identical for 8,000 years. What in your own life has been passed down basically unchanged?

stories.question: Why does Georgian writing look like nothing else?
stories.body: Georgian has its own ALPHABET, which is one of only 14 living scripts in active use on Earth today (compared to thousands of languages worldwide). The Georgian alphabet has 33 round, swooping letters that look like nothing else: "ა", "ბ", "გ" and so on. There are actually THREE traditional Georgian scripts (Asomtavruli, Nuskhuri and Mkhedruli), all considered cultural heritage. UNESCO recognised the living Georgian script in 2016. The script has been continuously written in Georgia for at least 1,500 years, possibly much longer. Every shop sign, road sign, restaurant menu and book in Georgia uses it. Many travellers say learning even a handful of Georgian letters makes the country feel completely different to read.

stories.question: What is a Georgian "supra"?
stories.body: A "supra" is the Georgian feast tradition. Long tables groan under enough food for three times the people. A "tamada" (toastmaster) leads the gathering with carefully composed toasts: to parents, to children, to friends present and absent, to peace, to the food, to the wine. Each toast is met with a slow drink and a deep silence. Sometimes Georgian polyphonic three-part harmony singing breaks out between toasts. A supra can last 4 to 8 hours. It is one of the deepest social rituals in any country and a window into how seriously Georgians take hospitality. Even a casual restaurant dinner can slip into supra-style toasts if a Georgian host is at your table.

stories.question: Why is Tbilisi called "warm place"?
stories.body: The name "Tbilisi" comes from the Georgian word "tbili", meaning "warm". The city sits over natural hot sulphur springs that bubble up from underground. According to legend, the king Vakhtang Gorgasali went hunting in the 5th century, his hunting falcon caught a pheasant in mid-air, and they BOTH fell into a steaming hot spring and were cooked. The king tasted the water, realised it was naturally hot, and decided to build his capital there. The Abanotubani sulphur baths still bubble in the city centre today, in beautiful brick-domed bathhouses where Georgians soak as they have for 1,500 years.

stories.question: What's Georgia's relationship with its big neighbour?
stories.body: Georgia spent much of the 20th century inside the Soviet Union (1921 to 1991), then regained independence when the Soviet Union collapsed. Modern Georgia's relationship with Russia is complicated. In 2008, a short war between Georgia and Russia ended with Russia recognising the breakaway regions of South Ossetia and Abkhazia as independent (most other countries do not). Around 20% of Georgia's internationally recognised territory is now controlled by these breakaway regions and Russian forces. Georgians today look mostly westward: they have signed an Association Agreement with the EU, and the majority want to eventually join the EU and NATO. Most travellers visit only the main parts of Georgia, where things are stable, peaceful and welcoming. The breakaway regions are essentially closed to tourism.

convoQuestions.question: Tbilisi, Caucasus, wine region, cave city, which has felt the most "Georgia"?
Did you eat khachapuri the proper way (mix the egg, butter and cheese with bread)?
How does the Georgian alphabet look to you, even after a few days?
Wine has been made the same way here for 8,000 years. What does that kind of continuity mean to you?

wordSearch.hint: Georgian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Brown bear
animals.description: Around 400 brown bears live in the Greater Caucasus mountains of Georgia, especially in Tusheti and Borjomi-Kharagauli national parks.

animals.name: Bearded vulture (lammergeier)
animals.description: A massive Caucasus vulture with a 3 m wingspan, glides on thermals over the high mountains.

animals.name: Tushetian shepherd dog
animals.description: A huge fluffy livestock guard dog used by Tushetian and Khevsureti shepherds. Looks like a small bear.

animals.name: Bezoar ibex
animals.description: A wild mountain goat with huge curved horns, living on the high crags of the Greater Caucasus.

animals.name: Grey wolf
animals.description: A small population in the high mountains, especially in Tusheti and along the Russian border.

animals.name: Caucasian leopard
animals.description: Critically endangered. Maybe 5-10 individuals left in Georgia, mostly in the south-eastern Vashlovani park. Camera-trap sightings only.

animals.name: Griffon vulture
animals.description: Big colonies nest on the cliffs of Vashlovani National Park. Soaring on thermals over the steppes.
